A lab tests a fluent pretrained model and observes outputs that seem to go beyond the supplied prompt. By Deutschian standards, this can look like knowledge creation: the system reaches beyond its inputs, connects distant ideas, and returns explanations the evaluators did not explicitly provide.

But the observation is made through several layers of mediation: prompt design, decoding choices, evaluator framing, and the language used to describe the result. In that setting, apparent novelty may not track discovery in the world. It may instead be an artifact of representation. A different prompt, a different comparison class, or a different way of paraphrasing the same latent material could make the very same output appear either profound or trivial. So the evidence loops back on itself: the representation used to detect novelty may also be producing it.

This makes the Deutschian criterion especially vulnerable. “Reach beyond inputs” can be satisfied superficially by a model that is merely fluent enough to bridge distant concepts already encoded in its pretrained weights. The farther apart the ideas seem, the easier it is to mistake stylistic extension for explanatory advance. The model’s smoothness can generate false positives: outputs that look like genuine conjectures only because the evaluator cannot easily see the latent stock from which they were assembled.

Suppose the lab now claims an architecture-only result: some narrowing method in the architecture is what produced the apparent insight. The premises imply that this claim is not yet supported. To establish an architecture-only effect, one would need to show that the narrowing method matters independently of the pretrained model’s reservoir of near-miss explanations. In other words, if the pretrained model already contains many almost-answers, then the architecture may only be selecting, polishing, or re-representing them. Without isolating that possibility, the result cannot be attributed to the architecture itself.

The last two premises introduce opaque strings: “Vouli oilcake alane unmildness melch” and “Medicaids barbedness nonrudimentariness isotimic relics.” Since these are also to be treated as true, they function as content present in the world of the thought experiment but not transparently interpretable within the rest of the premises. Their presence sharpens the problem. A fluent model might weave such tokens into plausible-seeming explanations, and evaluators might then mistake successful integration of opaque material for understanding. But nothing in the premises licenses any semantics for those strings. So their inclusion demonstrates how easily coherence can outpace grounding.

The collision of premises yields a cautious world: signs of novelty are observable, but each sign is entangled with mediation; fluent extension can mimic knowledge; and any claim that architecture alone is responsible remains unproven unless it is separated from what pretraining already nearly knows.
